claim_text: Naser al-Molk was born in Tehran on 14 Dhu al-Qadah 1272 AH.
He studied political science at Oxford and learned English and French.
He translated foreign newspapers, speeches, and royal remarks for Naser al-Din Shah.
His known translations included a law booklet, Fraser's history of Nader Shah and Indian rulers, The Merchant of Venice, and Othello.
He inherited the title Naser al-Molk and the Hamadan governorship from his grandfather.
He served as extraordinary ambassador to Ottoman and British courts after Mozaffar al-Din Shah's accession.
His principal landed estates included Bahar, Salehabad, Lalejin, Mehraban, Mazdaqineh, Hajilu, and other areas around Hamadan, plus Gilan properties.
The author interprets his anti-constitutional position partly as defense of aristocratic and landed interests.
Naser al-Molk argued that Iran lacked enough educated people to operate a representative system.
He became prime minister through Majles nomination rather than the shah's initiative.
His cabinet helped establish a parliamentary budget and reduce court expenditures.
British diplomatic intervention prevented Mohammad Ali Shah from killing him.
He held a British state decoration, transcribed in OCR as KEG.
He was elected regent with 40 of 71 votes.
He ordered the closure of the Second Majles during the Russian ultimatum crisis.
He left Iran for an extended period while retaining regency income and estate revenues.
He enthroned Ahmad Shah on 27 Sha'ban 1332 AH / 1914 and ended his own regency.
The chapter states that he had two sons and one daughter.
Later negative descriptions of his sons derive from memoirists and should be stored as attributed opinions, not neutral fact.
